elements.sent-2411-001: Over the last decades, observational cosmology has become a data-driven field of physics thanks to the huge leaps in our ability to observe the Universe through a multitude of observables.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this work, we are concerned with the observations of the distribution of galaxies in the Universe as observed by the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI).
elements.sent-2411-003: DESI is expected to observe a field of 14~000 $\deg^2$, the largest so far, by measuring the precise position of 40 million objects from the near Universe at $z\sim 0.1$ until $z\sim 2$ \citep{Snowmass2013.Levi,DESI2016a.Science,DESI2016b.Instr,DESI2022.KP1.Instr,FocalPlane.Silber.2023,Corrector.Miller.2023,Spectro.Pipeline.Guy.2023,SurveyOps.Schlafly.2023,Levi2019, DESI2023a,DESI2023a.KP1.SV,DESI2023b.KP1.EDR, DESI2023b , exceeding by far the observations of its predecessor the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) \citep{Eisenstein2011, Dawson2013 .}}
elements.sent-2411-004: The main objective of these spectroscopic surveys is to observe the largest possible amount of matter tracer positions in the largest possible cosmological volume to compute precise clustering measurements where Baryon Acoustic Oscillations (BAO) are detected.
elements.sent-2411-005: The BAO analysis has been used for 20 years \cite{Cole05, Eisenstein05, Beutler11, Alam2021 to deduce cosmological information from galaxy clustering and is known to be a robust method to constrain the expansion history of the Universe.}
elements.sent-2411-006: In addition, Full-Shape analyses allow us to constrain the growth of structure through cosmic history.
elements.sent-2411-007: DESI focuses on so-called “full-shape” analyses (e.g. \cite{Brieden21,DESI2024.V.KP5 ) which extract not only the information on Redshift Space Distortions, but also extracts information on the shape of the clustering, therefore shown to be more informative over the standard RSD analysis used in eBOSS \citep{Maus23 .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: In either case, observing the Universe at its largest scale presents an unavoidable challenge: we can only observe one universe.
elements.sent-2411-009: The absence of an ensemble of observable universes implies we cannot repeat our observations to estimate errors in our measurements and we are forced to rely on either simulated universes -- that we must make as realistic as possible -- or on analytical estimates of the covariances of our measurements.
elements.sent-2411-010: This paper aims to compare the analytical estimates of the covariance matrix against the ``true'' sample covariance matrix.
elements.sent-2411-011: We do this by generating analytical matrices based on the mock sample itself -- as opposed to generating them from the data --, thereby removing the assumption that the distribution of mocks is the same distribution underlying the data.
elements.sent-2411-012: In particular, we compare both techniques directly (i.e. the matrices themselves) and in terms of their impact on the BAO and the full shape fits of DESI.
elements.sent-2411-013: These studies allow us to detect any potential bias in the parameter or error estimates due to the covariance matrix choice.
elements.sent-2411-014: This work is part of the set of supporting papers around the DESI BAO \cite{DESI2024.III.KP4 and Full-Shape \cite{DESI2024.V.KP5 analyses using the DESI Data Release 1 (DR1) \cite{DESI2024.I.DR1 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Other relevant papers using DR1 include the clustering measurements from galaxies and quasars, \cite{DESI2024.II.KP3 , the Lyman-$\alpha$ forest results \cite{DESI2024.IV.KP6 as well as the cosmological results from BAO \cite{DESI2024.VI.KP7A and full-shape \cite{DESI2024.VII.KP7B .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: This paper is organised as follows: in \cref{sec:methods we briefly introduce the methods used in this comparison.}
elements.sent-2411-017: First regarding the fitting techniques and relevant cosmological parameters and second, the estimation of sample and analytical covariances in configuration and Fourier space.
elements.sent-2411-018: Then, \cref{sec:results reports the results of our tests, with \cref{sec:conf-results focusing on configuration-space-based measurements and BAO analyses whereas \cref{sec:fourier-results focuses on Fourier-space measurements and Full-Shape analyses.}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Finally, we discuss and conclude in \cref{sec:discussion-conclusion .}
elements.sent-2411-020: This work relies on the existence of a large sample of mock Universes (or ``mocks'') that are realistic enough to reproduce the observed galaxy samples; which could then be assumed to have been drawn from the same underlying probability distribution as the mocks.
elements.sent-2411-021: However, generating a large sample of precise $N$-body mocks is a computational challenge, as DESI not only observed a much larger effective cosmological volume than its predecessors, but did so with significantly greater precision.
elements.sent-2411-022: To alleviate this, various approaches have emerged in which we trade precision in the gravitational evolution of the matter field and accuracy at small scales for computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-023: This trade-off enables the generation of a large number of mocks, which are required to build accurate mock-based covariance matrices.
elements.sent-2411-024: We refer the reader to refs.~\cite{Chuang2015, Lippich2019, Colavincenzo2019 for comprehensive studies of the various methods to generate these approximate mocks.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Throughout this work, we use the EZmock\footnote{\url{https://github.com/cheng-zhao/EZmock method \cite{Chuang2015EZmock, Zhao2021 , which combines the computational efficiency of the Zeldovich approximation-based \citep{Zeldovich1970 gravitational evolution of the dark matter (DM) field with a flexible bias model and probability distribution function (PDF) mapping scheme that aims at fitting not only the reference two-point statistics on large scales, but also the bispectrum.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Thanks to its speed and flexibility, EZmock was the baseline method to construct covariance matrices for the eBOSS Collaboration cosmological analyses \cite{Zhao2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: In what follows we give a short description of the EZmock generation and processing to reproduce the observed DESI galaxy samples.
elements.sent-2411-028: For a full description of the mock suite, we refer the reader to Ref.~\cite{KP3s8-Zhao .}
elements.sent-2411-029: The EZmock suite consists of a set of 1000 North and an equal number of South Galactic Cap pseudo-lightcone\footnote{The $\Delta z$ between snapshots is large and the snapshots were joined for processing and addition of Fiber Assignment Effects.}
elements.sent-2411-030: We do not use mocks with actual redshift evolution.
elements.sent-2411-031: For each tracer and redshift snapshot, 2000 $(6\Gpch)^3$ boxes of $1728^3$ DM particles were generated with independent Gaussian initial conditions.
elements.sent-2411-032: The resulting DM fields were converted to galaxy fields via the bias model, which is tuned snapshot-by-snapshot to the two-point clustering of the DESI Abacus HOD mocks (i.e. galaxies, see \cite{Yuan2024, antoine2023 ), which were in turn fit to the data from the DESI one-percent survey \citep{DESI2023b.KP1.EDR .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: These boxes were then converted to sky geometry.
elements.sent-2411-034: Note that, due to the large volume of the boxes, there is no volume replication required to fit one entire galactic cap.
elements.sent-2411-035: This is designed to avoid mis-estimating the cosmic variance.
elements.sent-2411-036: The different snapshots are then concatenated in a single pseudo-lightcone into which the DESI Y1 footprint and Fast Fibre Assignment (FFA) \cite{KP3s11-Sikandar are applied to enhance the realism of the catalogues.}
elements.sent-2411-037: A summary of the snapshots used for each of the DESI tracers can be found in \cref{tab:mocks .}

elements.sent-2411-038: Given an ensemble of $N_{\rm mocks}$ measurements of a given $N_{\rm bins}$ bin-discretised statistic $\{S_i(x_j)\}_{i=0,j=0 ^{N_{\rm mocks ,N_{\rm bins }$, the sample covariance is estimated as \begin{equation}\label{eq:sample-cov-def} \vb{C}_{{\rm s},ij} = \frac{1}{N_{\rm mocks} - 1}\sum_{k=1}^{N_{\rm mocks}} \left(S_k(x_i) - \bar{S}(x_i)\right)\left(S_k(x_j) - \bar{S}(x_j)\right),\end{equation} where, in our case, $S(x)$ is either the concatenated configuration space correlation function (2PCF) multipoles ($[\xi_{0 (s),\xi_{2 (s)]$) or power spectrum multipoles ($[P_{0 (k),P_{2 (k)]$).}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-044: In this comparison we test both an idealised case, in which the analytical covariances are tuned to the mean variance of the complete mock clustering measurement sample, and the realistic case in which the covariances are generated from a single mock realisation and the noise is estimated using jackknife.
elements.sent-2411-045: It must be stressed that the analytical covariances have been tuned to the mock clustering (instead of data); therefore, we expect them to accurately match the sample covariance estimate.

elements.sent-2411-048: The covariance matrix of the two-point correlation function of a single tracer can in general be written as \begin{equation}\vb{C}(r,\mu,r',\mu') \equiv \mathrm{cov}\left[\xi(r,\mu), \xi(r',\mu')\right] = \langle\xi(r,\mu)\xi(r',\mu')\rangle - \langle\xi(r,\mu)\rangle\langle\xi(r',\mu')\rangle\end{equation} and involves contributions from quads of points, some of which may coincide, which are then related to the 4-, 3- and 2-point correlation functions.
elements.sent-2411-049: This expression can be expanded into integrals that \rascalc{ \cite{rascal,rascal-jackknife,rascalC,rascalC-legendre-3 performs using importance sampling of groups of 2, 3 and 4-point groups from random catalogues generated for the survey volume; this ensures that the survey geometry is automatically taken into account in the integration.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: A key feature of the \rascalc{ method is that instead of computing the exact non-Gaussian contributions, the Gaussian terms\footnote{Gaussian means based solely on the 2-point function.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: The 2-point function input to \rascalc{ is typically empirical and thus includes non-linearities.}
elements.sent-2411-052: It is introduced through the modified shot-noise approximation (reducing the square of the overdensity appearing in the 3- and 2-point contributions): \begin{equation}\langle\delta_i^2\rangle \approx \frac{\alpha_{\rm SN}}{n_i V_{\rm cell}}(1 + \delta_i),\end{equation} where $\alpha_{\rm SN =1$ corresponds to the standard, Poissonian shot noise, $\delta_i$ and $n_i$ are the overdensity and the number density in cell $i$, and $V_{\rm cell}$ is the volume of this cell; the grid is chosen to be fine enough so that each cell contains at most 1 random point.}
elements.sent-2411-053: This approach has been shown to successfully mimic non-Gaussian effects in the covariance matrix, while the computations themselves remain Gaussian.
elements.sent-2411-054: The shot-noise parameter can be tuned with a sample covariance (from mocks) or a jackknife covariance matrix directly from the data (we use both in this work).
elements.sent-2411-055: The \rascalc{ method has already been successfully used in the DESI context for the BAO analysis of the early data\footnote{``Early DESI data'' designates the first two months of the main survey.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: It is part of the upcoming Data Release 1 \citep{DESI2024.I.DR1 and should not be confused with the Early Data Release \citep{DESI2023b.KP1.EDR .. \cite{Moon2023,Misha2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: For further details on the features of the latest implementation of \rascalc{ we refer the reader to Ref.~\cite{KP4s7-Rashkovetskyi .}}

elements.sent-2411-071: The increased precision of the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) motivates a revisit of the modelling and fitting procedure of the BAO signal.
elements.sent-2411-072: A comprehensive study of these techniques and their impact on the error budget of the experiment is presented in Ref.~\cite{KP4s2-Chen .}
elements.sent-2411-073: We briefly summarise here the fitting methodology and highlight a few of the improvements over the legacy modelling strategy.
elements.sent-2411-074: BAO fits are performed on reconstructed configuration space measurements, i.e. the two first multipoles ($\ell=0,\,2$) of the two-point correlation function $\xi_\ell(s)$.
elements.sent-2411-075: BAO-reconstruction is a post-processing step that moves observed galaxies ``back in time'' using a (Zeldovich) approximated displacement field, estimated using the Iterative Fast Fourier Transform (IFFT) method first presented in Ref.~\cite{Burden2015 and optimised for DESI in \cite{KP4s3-Chen, KP4s4-Paillas .}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The systematic tests and hyperparameter choices for the method are described in Ref.~\cite{KP4s3-Chen,KP4s4-Paillas .}
elements.sent-2411-077: The eBOSS analyses used a ``RecIso'' reconstruction convention, where the galaxies are moved back and RSD is approximately removed, whereas for the random sample no RSD-removal term is used.
elements.sent-2411-078: As a result, the final clustering will show little to no RSD (quadrupole) signal.
elements.sent-2411-079: DESI 2024 analyses, on the other hand, use the ``RecSym'' reconstruction convention \cite{White2015 -- which stands for symmetric reconstruction -- where both data and randoms are treated in the same way, that is, they are displaced with the same field (including the RSD term), which preserves the quadrupole signal.}
elements.sent-2411-080: We measure the dilations of the BAO scale in the parallel ($\alpha_\parallel$) and perpendicular ($\alpha_\perp$) directions, with respect to a template or fiducial cosmology.
elements.sent-2411-081: This means the relevant BAO parameters are interpreted as \begin{equation}\alpha_\parallel = \frac{H^{\rm fid}(z)}{H(z)}\frac{r_d^{\rm fid}}{r_d}, \alpha_\perp = \frac{D_A(z)}{D_A^{\rm fid}(z)}\frac{r_d^{\rm fid}}{r_d},\end{equation} where $H^{(\rm fid) (z)$ denotes the Hubble parameter, $D_A^{(\rm fid) (z)$ the angular diameter distance, $r_d$ the sound horizon at the drag epoch and the superscript ${(\rm fid)}$ denotes quantities computed assuming a fiducial cosmology.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: In practice, the template is modified by the scaling of the (observed) Fourier space coordinates $(k,\mu)$ as \begin{equation}k' = \frac{k}{\alpha_\perp}\left[1 + \mu^2\left(\frac{\alpha_\parallel^2}{\alpha_\perp^2} - 1\right)\right]^{1/2} \mu' = \mu\frac{\alpha_\perp}{\alpha_\parallel}\left[1 + \mu^2\left(\frac{\alpha_\parallel^2}{\alpha_\perp^2} - 1\right)\right]^{1/2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-083: One of the major changes in the revised BAO modelling for DESI is that, even for a configuration space, the $\alpha$ scaling is performed directly in the Fourier-space template, instead of being applied to the model correlation function -- as was previously the case -- (see equation 2 in Ref.~\cite{Xu2012 ).}
elements.sent-2411-084: While the relevant dilation parameters are $\alpha_{\perp,\parallel}$, we will more often show results in terms of the strictly anisotropic $\alpha_{\rm ap \equiv \alpha_\parallel/\alpha_\perp$ and isotropic $\alpha_{\rm iso}\equiv \alpha_\parallel^{1/3}\alpha_\perp^{2/3}$ dilations.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Similarly to the legacy approach, the template relies on the separation of the wiggle ($P_{\rm w}$) and non-wiggle ($P_{\rm nw}$) component of the power spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-086: In a general form, this is written as \begin{equation}P_{gg,\ell}(k) = \frac{2\ell + 1}{2}\int_{-1}^1\dd\mu \left[B(k,\mu)P_{\rm nw}(k) + C(k,\mu)P_{\rm w}(k)\right]L_\ell(\mu) \xi_{gg,\ell}(s) = i^\ell\int\frac{\dd k}{2\pi^2} k^2 P_{gg,\ell}(k)j_\ell(ks) + \tilde{D}_\ell(s)\end{equation} For 2D BAO fits we adopt the RecSym reconstruction convention (see refs.~\cite{KP4s3-Chen, KP4s4-Paillas for details on reconstruction algorithms and conventions), such that \begin{equation}B(k, \mu) = \left(b + f\mu^2\right)^2\left[1 + \frac{1}{2}k^2\mu^2\Sigma_s^2\right]^{-2}, C(k, \mu) = \left(b + f\mu^2\right)^2\exp{-\frac{1}{2}k^2\left[\mu^2\Sigma_\parallel^2 + (1-\mu)^2\Sigma_\perp^2\right]}, \tilde{D}_0(s) = \tilde{a}_{0,0} + \tilde{a}_{0,1}\left(\frac{sk_{\rm min}}{2\pi}\right)^2, \tilde{D}_2(s) = \tilde{a}_{2,0} + \tilde{a}_{2,1}\left(\frac{sk_{\rm min}}{2\pi}\right)^2 + \Delta^3\left[a_{2,0}B_{2,0}(s\Delta)+a_{2,1}B_{2,1}(s\Delta)\right].\end{equation}.}

elements.section-2411-004: Constructing covariance matrix for DESI Year 1 Full Shape analyses
elements.sent-2411-179: The tests shown throughout this paper have led us to conclude that the \thecov method of generating analytical covariance matrices for Fourier space clustering does not properly capture the variance in the mocks used, which could be due to a number of factors such as the Fiber Assignment method used in the mocks, the very complex sky footprint of the Year 1 DESI data and how systematics are taken into account (i.e. weighting).
elements.sent-2411-180: We refer the reader to Ref. \cite{KP4s8-Alves for an in-depth study of these phenomena, considering clustering and fibre assignment approximations in our fast simulations.}
elements.sent-2411-181: Ultimately, these findings do not yet allow us to recommend analytic covariances over mock-based ones in Fourier space for the full-shape analyses.
elements.sent-2411-182: However, mock-based covariance matrices have been found not to represent DESI DR1 perfectly either.
elements.sent-2411-183: Goodness-of-fit tests with the BAO model on DESI data have indicated that the \ezmock covariances underestimate the errors by $\approx 10\%$, despite the 2-point clustering matching between the mocks and data \citep{DESI2024.II.KP3 .}
elements.sent-2411-184: The potential reasons are discrepancies in 3- and 4-point clustering and the inaccuracies of the Fast Fiber Assign (FFA) algorithm applied to \ezmock.
elements.sent-2411-185: The best-fit chi-squared values are much closer to expected with \rascalc{ configuration-space covariance matrices tuned on data (as opposed to the mock-tuned \rascalc{ used throughout this work).}}
elements.sent-2411-186: To correct this, we have decided to rescale the mock-based covariance matrices for Full-Shape analysis by a sample-specific factor based on configuration-space comparison with data-tuned \rascalc{ results.}
elements.sent-2411-187: We use the mean ``reduced chi-squared'' measure from Ref.~\cite{Misha2023,KP4s7-Rashkovetskyi : \begin{equation}\label{eq:chi2_red-definition} \chi^2_{\rm red} \qty({\bf C}_R^{-1}, {\bf C}_{\rm s}) \equiv \frac1{N_{\rm bins}} \tr({\bf C}_R^{-1} {\bf C}_{\rm s}),\end{equation} where ${\bf C _R$ is a \rascalc{ covariance matrix tuned on data, ${\bf C _{\rm s}$ is a configuration-space \ezmock sample covariance (\cref{eq:sample-cov-def ) and $N_{\rm bins}$ is the dimension of both matrices.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\label{eq:chi2_red-definition} \chi^2_{\rm red} \qty({\bf C}_R^{-1}, {\bf C}_{\rm s}) \equiv \frac1{N_{\rm bins}} \tr({\bf C}_R^{-1} {\bf C}_{\rm s}),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-188: If the \rascalc{ covariance matrix describes the distribution of \ezmock clustering measurements, the ``reduced chi-squared'' value is expected to be 1 with a small errorbar \cite{Misha2023,KP4s7-Rashkovetskyi .}}
elements.sent-2411-189: Thus we can bring the mock-based covariance into closer agreement with the \rascalc{ result by multiplying the mock covariance matrix by $1/\chi^2_{\rm red \qty({\bf C _R^{-1 , {\bf C _{\rm s )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: The ``reduced chi-squared'' values only vary a few per cent depending on the separation range and the number of multipoles \cite{DESI2024.II.KP3 .}
elements.sent-2411-191: The final values of the correction factor vary from 10 to 40\% depending on the sample.
elements.sent-2411-192: The actual values for all tracers and redshift bins can be found in Ref. \cite{DESI2024.V.KP5,DESI2024.II.KP3 .}
elements.sent-2411-193: DESI DR1 full shape analysis (\cite{DESI2024.V.KP5 ) therefore adopted the sample covariance matrix rescaled with this factor.}
elements.sent-2411-194: In this work, we have compared analytic estimates of the covariance matrix for two-point clustering measurements in configuration and Fourier space against the corresponding sample covariance.
elements.sent-2411-195: This was achieved first by generating analytic covariance matrices using the mock sample itself, instead of the observed data.
elements.sent-2411-196: In doing this we avoid the assumption that mocks and data are drawn from the same underlying distribution and therefore make our results independent of the mock-generation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-197: Moreover, we do not limit our tests to visual comparisons between matrix elements, but perform cosmological analyses on the whole sample of mocks to deduce the effects of the differences in covariance matrices in terms of parameter estimates and their uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-198: In particular, we focus on the fiducial DESI 2024 analysis pipeline, which focuses on 2D Baryon Acoustic Oscillations (BAO) fits using the configuration space two-point function measurements of BAO-reconstructed galaxy samples and Full-Shape fits using Fourier-space measurements of pre-reconstruction samples.
elements.sent-2411-199: For our BAO-based configuration space tests we have used the \rascalc{ technique and find that parameter constraints obtained with the analytical estimates are consistent with the corresponding parameters obtained when using the sample covariance.}
elements.sent-2411-200: In addition, we obtain no significant biases on the error estimates either.
elements.sent-2411-201: These results apply to matrices tuned to individual mock realisations (as would be done for the data) as well as those tuned on the full mock sample.
elements.sent-2411-202: We conclude that given a single clustering measurement (i.e. DESI data), the \rascalc{ covariance generated from it yields good estimates of the errors on cosmological parameters and was used as the covariance matrix of the baseline DESI BAO analysis on DR1 data \citep{DESI2024.III.KP4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-203: On the contrary, we find from Fourier-space testing that the mock and analytical covariance matrix estimates show significant differences (around 10\%) not only in an element-to-element comparison but that these large discrepancies induce equally significant differences in the error estimates of cosmological parameters.
elements.sent-2411-204: We have tested both ShapeFit and direct fit Full-Shape analysis techniques and found biases in some of the parameter errors and $\chi^2$ as large as 12\%.
